coredata.dc:description: Herein we describe the facile synthesis of new N-doped carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) obtained from 1,10-phenanthroline by the solvothermal method. Characterization of CNPs were carried out with transmission electron microscope (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS), Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR), UV–vis absorption spectra, and luminescence spectra. CNPs were pH sensitive and exploited as fluorescent chemosensors and imaging agents for Al(III) and Zn(II) ions in real-life samples. Remarkably, we show that CNPs can be used for the detection of Al(III) and Zn(II) ions in water samples. Accordingly, the results indicate that CNPs are highly effective in detecting Zn(II) content of cosmetic creams. We also demonstrated that the CNPs could be used for in vitro imaging of Al(III) and Zn(II) in Human Larynx Squamous Cell Carcinoma (Hep-2). Finally, Al(III) imaging in Angelica Officinalis root tissue was also achieved successfully. The CNPs are promising as luminescent multianalyte (pH, Al(III) and Zn(II)) sensors.

(A) UV–vis absorption, (B) excitation (λem = 360 nm), and (C) fluorescence (λex = 270 nm) spectra of the CNPs in PBS solution (pH 7.4). (D) Fluorescence spectra of CNPs (3.32 μg/ml) under excitation with different wavelengths in PBS (pH 7.4). Stability of CNPs (E) under irradiation with white LEDs in PBS (pH 7.4). (F) in NaCl solution.
Ph-dependent fluorescence spectra (λex = 300 nm) of CNPs (3.32 μg/ml).

(A) Fluorescence spectra, and (B) relative fluorescence intensity of CNPs (3.32 μg/ml) in the presence of Al3+, Zn2+, Ag+, Au3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Li+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Pt2+, K+,and Na+ (3.33 × 10−6 M) in PBS buffer (pH 7.4). Fluorescence spectra of CNPs (3.32 μg/ml) in the presence of C) Al(III) (0.0–3.33 × 10−6 M)) and D) Zn(II) (0.0–1.66 × 10−6 M) in PBS buffer (pH 7.4, λex = 300 nm).

Reversibility test for CNPs. Fluorescence spectra of CNPs (3.32 μg/ml) upon sequential addition of (A) Al(III) (3.33 × 10−6 M), and EDTA (3.33 × 10−6 M) (B) Zn(II) (1.66 × 10−6 M), and EDTA (1.66 × 10−6 M) in PBS buffer (pH 7.4, λex = 300 nm).

Fluorescence spectra of CNPs (3.32 μg/ml) upon sequential addition of interferents (Ag+, Au3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Li+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Pt2+, K+, andNa+) and (A) Al(III) (3.33 × 10−6 M) and (B) Zn(II) (1.66 × 10−6 M) in PBS buffer (pH 7.4, λλex = 300 nm).
Fluorescence spectra of CNPs (3.32 μg/ml) with Zn+2 ions (2.32 × 10−6 M) in cosmetic cream (PBS, pH 7.40, λex = 300 nm).
Hep-2 cells incubated with CNPs, A) alone, and in the presence of B) Al(III) and C) Zn(II). Scale bar = 20 μm, excitation filters: 340–380 nm.
Fluorescence microscopy images of Angelica Officinalis root (A) alone, and after incubation with (B) CNPs, (C) CNPs and Al(III). Scale bar = 20 μm, excitation filters: 340–380 nm.
Synthesis and fluorescence response of CNPs to pH, Al(III), and Zn(II) ions, and real-life applications.

head.ce:title: N-doped carbon nanomaterials as fluorescent pH and metal ion sensors for imaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: N-doped carbon nanomaterials, which can be used as pH, Al(III) and Zn(II) sensors and imaging agents are described.

Herein we describe the facile synthesis of new N-doped carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) obtained from 1,10-phenanthroline by the solvothermal method. Characterization of CNPs were carried out with transmission electron microscope (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS), Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR), UV–vis absorption spectra, and luminescence spectra. CNPs were pH sensitive and exploited as fluorescent chemosensors and imaging agents for Al(III) and Zn(II) ions in real-life samples. Remarkably, we show that CNPs can be used for the detection of Al(III) and Zn(II) ions in water samples. Accordingly, the results indicate that CNPs are highly effective in detecting Zn(II) content of cosmetic creams. We also demonstrated that the CNPs could be used for in vitro imaging of Al(III) and Zn(II) in Human Larynx Squamous Cell Carcinoma (Hep-2). Finally, Al(III) imaging in Angelica Officinalis root tissue was also achieved successfully. The CNPs are promising as luminescent multianalyte (pH, Al(III) and Zn(II)) sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based nanomaterials such as graphene, graphene oxide, fullerenes, activated carbon, carbon nanotubes, graphitic carbon nitride and carbon dots have recently emerged as promising nanomaterials [1–7] for sensing, biomedical, energy, catalyst and environmental applications [8,9]. Carbon nanomaterials have several advantages such as high stability, tunable structure, adjustable optical, thermal and electrical features, high surface area, and high charge carrier mobility [10]. Furthermore, some of these nanomaterials can be obtained from cheap starting compounds by cost-effective and straightforward synthetic routes [11]. In particular, they represent an important class of sustainable and biocompatible fluorophores with low toxicity and an environmentally benign nature [11,12]. Therefore, carbon nanomaterials can be used in various applications, including sensing, imaging, drug delivery, therapy, catalysis, and anticounterfeiting [5,8,13–19]. Therefore, development of new carbon nanomaterials is a hot research topic.

pH is vital for most biological processes. Indeed, pH fluctuations indicate many pathological situations, including cancer, inflammation, infection, ischemia, cystic fibrosis, kidney failure, and immune system disorders [20,21]. Thus, the information about health and disease-related conditions can be gathered by determining the pH. Hence, considerable efforts have been devoted to the design and synthesis of pH sensors. Luminescence is one of the most valuable techniques since it is simple, rapid, reproducible, and inexpensive. Furthermore, it provides wide dynamic ranges and high spatiotemporal resolution in bioimaging [22]. To date, several pH-responsive luminescent carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) have been reported [23–25].

Although some metal ions play key roles in biological processes, the redundant accumulation of most metal ions in the body may lead to severe pathological conditions [26]. Amongst metal ions, aluminum is widely utilized in food, agriculture, cosmetic, automobile, pharmaceutical, metallurgy, aerospace, and building construction industries [27,28]. Frequent usages of aluminum or its alloys in our daily life may result in the release of aluminum ions in the environment. The gradual accumulation of Al(III) ions led to toxic effects in many living organisms. It can also deteriorate the ecological environment and inhibit the growth of animals and plants [29]. In humans, Al(III) can bind to phosphates of DNA and RNA [27], mainly affecting the nervous system, thus causing Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s diseases [30,31]. Furthermore, Al(III) can cause breast cancer, renal failure, dementia, and even death [32–34]. The Al(III) level in drinking water should not exceed 0.2 mg/L [28]. The World Health Organization (WHO) does not permit dietary intake of Al(III) more than 7 mg/kg of a person per week [28,30,31]. Therefore, it is critical to develope new chemosensors for detecting and quantification of Al(III) ions in biological, chemical and environmental samples. To date, many fluorescent chemosensors for Al(III) have been reported [26,29–33,35,36].

Zn(II) is an abundant and vital ion for humans since it plays key roles in biological processes. Intracellular free or bound Zn(II) concentration varies between picomolar to micromolar levels.[22] Zn(II) regulates brain function, apoptosis, signal transmission, immune and enzyme functions, and gene expression.[37,38] Besides, any breakdown in Zn(II) homeostasis may result in serious pathological conditions like Alzheimer’s, Parkinson’s diseases, cancer, diabetes, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, ischemic stroke and epilepsy.[22,35,39,40] Therefore, accurate location and concentration of Zn(II) should be determined to understand the biology of Zn(II) clearly. Unfortunately, detecting Zn(II) ions can be challenging due to their electronic configuration, which hampers the use of diverse spectroscopic methods for this silent metal ion. Thankfully, fluorescence spectroscopy offers high compatibility, sensitivity, and simplicity for detecting Zn(II) ions.

The binding of ions to receptor ligands increases the molecular rigidity and abolishes photoinduced electron transfer or disturbs the charge transfer processes, giving rise to modulated fluorescence signals.[41] Based on this principle, many examples of fluorescent pH and metal ion chemosensors have been reported. [35,37–39,41,42] However, most of the chemosensors developed for pH, Al(III) or Zn(II) are based on organic molecules and have some intrinsic limitations. These limitations are: (i) poor solubility in water, (ii) multistep synthesis, and (iii) limited sensitivity. In this regard, chemosensors based on carbon nanomaterials are appealing candidates since they offer (i) water solubility, (ii) ease of synthesis from abundant starting materials in a single step and high sensitivity.[28,34,43–45] Nevertheless, chemosensors that can induce a fluorescent response to both pH and metal ions simultaneously have been rare.[46–48].

From our endeavors in the chemosensor arena,[38,49–58] we herein wish to report the facile synthesis of new N-doped CNPs. The CNPs were produced from 1,10-phenanthroline by solvothermal method. Previously, we have reported fluorescent chemosensors based on the 1,10-phenanthroline scaffold. [24,35,38] Our work clearly showed that 1,10-phenanthroline was highly responsive to some certain metal ions including Zn(II), Cd(II) or Fe(II). Here, the metal ions were coordinated with the nitrogens of 1,10-phenanthroline to give well-defined complexes, having different HOMO and LUMO levels and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) processes when compared to metal free systems. Thus, coordination of metal ions to the nitrogens of 1,10-phenanthroline changed the intensity of the fluorescence signal dramatically by inhibiting the PET process. Therefore, we selected this precursor for the synthesis of CNPs. We have envisaged that CNPs produced from 1,10-phenanthroline precursor would also have promising features as chemosensors. CNPs were characterized by traditional spectroscopic techniques; transmission electron microscope (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS), Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR), UV–vis absorption spectra, and luminescence spectra. Remarkably, CNPs induce a fluorescence response to pH, Al(III), and Zn(II). Furthermore, the CNPs were tested as chemosensors and imaging agents for Al(III) and Zn(II) ions in real-life samples. We have successfully demonstrated that CNPs could be used for the detection of Al(III) and Zn(II) ions in water samples. Accordingly, the results attested that CNPs are highly versatile in the detection of Zn(II) content of cosmetic creams. Finally, we showed that the CNPs were promising for in vitro imaging of Al(III) and Zn(II) in Human Larynx Squamous Cell Carcinoma (Hep-2). Strikingly, Al(III) imaging in Angelica Officinalis root tissue can also be achieved (Chart 1 ).

Chemicals were purchased from commercial sources and used as received unless otherwise noted: 1,10-Phenanthroline monohydrate (Sigma-Aldrich, 99 %), ethanol (Sigma-Aldrich, 96 %), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt dihydrate (EDTA) Sigma-Aldrich, 99 %), perchlorate salts of all metal ions (≥98 %) were used in spectrophotometric measurements except for Al(III), which was used as aluminum nitrate nonahydrate. Cosmetic creams and Angelica officinalis roots were bought from local suppliers. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by Varian Cary 50 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence measurements were recorded with Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer with a PMT voltage of 800 V. The morphology of the samples was observed by a transmission electron microscope (TEM) operating at 300 kV. Infrared spectra (FTIR) were obtained from a Nicolet iS5 FT-IR Spectrometer with iD5-ATR apparatus. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were acquired using an XPS, PHI 5000 VersaProbe, Physical Electronics with a monochromatic Al Kα irradiation source. The fluorescence imaging experiments were performed using a Leica fluorescence microscope. Al3+ and Zn2+ measurements in this study were performed using Thermo X Series 2 ICP-MS. The particle size was measured by Malvern Nano ZS90 Zetasizer (Malvern Instruments ltd., Malvern, U.K). Raman spectra were recorded with a Horiba Scientific Raman spectrometer and 633 nm laser was used as the light source.

CNPs were synthesized by solvothermal method. 1,10-Phenanthroline monohydrate (20 mg) was dissolved in ethanol and water mixture (1:1, v/v, 10 ml) in an ultrasonic bath for 30 min. The solution was sealed in a Teflon lined container equipped with a stainless-steel jacket and then heated up to 160 °C for 12 h. After cooling down naturally to room temperature, the brown mixture was centrifuged (9000 rpm for 10 min) to remove large particles. The supernatant was dialyzed against pure water using a dialysis bag (3400 Da) for 24 h. In the end, the CNPs were obtained by freeze-drying under vacuum and stored at + 4 °C until use.

Freshly prepared metal ion solutions (1.0 × 10−3M) from the corresponding perchlorate salts [with the exceptions of Al3+which was prepared from Al(NO3)3] were used in all measurements. CNPs(1 mg/ml) were dissolved in 0.01 M PBS buffer (pH 7.4) at room temperature. Certain amounts of freshly prepared metal ion solutions were added separately to the solution of CNPs. Final concentration of CNPs was 3.32 μg/ml, and 33.22 μg/ml for fluorescence and absorption measurements, respectively. 10 μL or 100 μL of the stock solution of CNPs (1 mg/ml) were added to a quartz cuvette containing 3 ml solvent (PBS or water) for fluorescence and absorption measurements, respectively. The slit width was 5 nm for fluorescence measurements.
Fluorescence quantum yield was calculated by using quinine as standard (Φ = 54 %) according to published methods[59] by following equation below.[60].
Φx = Φst (Gradx / Gradst)(η2 x/η2 st).

Excitation wavelength was 300 nm for quinine (Φ = 54 % in 0.1 N H2SO4 solution), and CNPs. Where Φ is the quantum yield, Grad is the gradient from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity vs absorbance and η is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript st and × are denoted to standard and CNPs, respectively.

ICP-MS analysis were carried out for the determination Zn(II) content of commercial cosmetic creams.[42] 0.5 g of cosmetic cream sample was weighed and dissolved in concentrated nitric and perchloric acids (4: 1, v/v) in a ventilated hood. The mixture was heated about 2–4 h until white fumes were observed. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was filtered, and diluted several times for ICP-MS analysis. The Zn(II) ion was quantitatively determined by ICP-MS and certain amounts of the stock solutions were used in luminescence analysis. The results of ICP-MS and luminescence were compared which were in good agreement as tabulated in Table S3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell imaging experiments with CNPs in the presence of Al(III) and Zn(II)
ce:section.ce:para: Human Larynx Squamous Cell Carcinoma (Hep-2) were seeded on 48 well plate at density of 25 × 105 incubated overnight at 37 °C under 5 % CO2 in air. Afterwards, CNPs (1, 5, 10, 25, 50 μg) were incubated for 12 h before addition of Al(III) or Zn(II) solutions. Next, the fluorescence images were obtained with a fluorescence microscope (Leica) from cells incubated with Al(III) and Zn(II). There was almost no fluorescence in the absence of metal ions. Scale bars of fluorescence images are 20 μm. The excitation filters were 340–380 nm for fluorescence imaging experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plant cell imaging of Al(III) in Angelica officinalis
ce:section.ce:para: Three groups of sliced Angelica officinalis roots were used for plant cell imaging experiments.[29] The first group was the control which included the roots without CNPs or metal ions. The second group of roots were soaked in a solution of CNPs (1 mg/ml) for 30 min. and dried in air for 1 h at ambient temperature. The last group was first soaked in a solution of CNPs (1 mg/ml) and then incubated with Al(III) solution (1 × 10−3 M) for 30 min. All the samples were analyzed and imaged with an inverted fluorescent microscope (Leica) equipped with a camera.

CNPs were obtained from 1,10 phenanthroline using a solvothermal method at 160 °C for 12 h. After dialysis for 24 h against water and freeze-drying, the obtained CNPs were characterized by spectroscopic techniques. Fig. 1 A shows the TEM images of CNPs, which display uniform spherical morphology with good dispersion. Based on TEM images, the average particle size of CNPs was 85 nm. DLS analysis was also performed to determine the hydrodynamic diameter of CNPs in an aqueous solution. It was found that CNPs had average diameter of 103 nm with a polydispersity index of 0.55 (Fig. 1B). The size of our CNPs is rather big compared with the typical values reported for carbon nanodots. It should be noted that polymeric precursors can produce large carbon nanoparticles.[61–64] Interestingly, it is reported that carbohydrates (glucose, sucrose, starch, chitosan or hyaluronic acid) could give carbon nanoparticles with diameters of 60–100 nm or even 260–320 nm.[65–67] Furthermore, Guo et. al. synthesized fluorescent carbon nitride nanoparticles with diameters 40–60 nm.[68] It is reasonable to assume that the structure of our precursor (1,10-phenanthroline) and the reaction time might be critical in the synthesis [69,70] and provide large carbon nanoparticles. The zeta potential (ζ-) of CNPs was + 11.4 mV (Fig. 1B inset). The ζ- potential indicated that CNPs had a positive surface charge density. Positively charged nanoparticles are advantageous for biological applications. Because many proteins, DNA and cell membrane surface are slightly anionic. So, positively charged nanoparticles are beneficial for enhanced loading efficiency and effective accumulation in the cells. In vitro results also showed that CNPs easily permeated into the cells probably due to their positive surface charge (vide infra). The chemical composition of CNPs was determined by employing XPS spectra. The XPS spectrum is depicted in Fig. 1C. The XPS spectrum shows three prominent peaks at 2.60 eV, 2.39 eV, and 3.25 eV, corresponding to distinct peaks of C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively. The percentage of N-doping was 9.88 % in CNPs. Finally, the functional groups of CNPs were elucidated with FTIR spectra (Fig. 1D). The peaks at 1691 cm−1, 1510 cm−1, and 1427 cm−1 represent stretching and bending vibrations for CC, CN, and CN bonds in CNPs, respectively. The Raman spectra of CNPs (Fig. 1E) exhibited two peaks, which could be attributed to sp3 and sp2 carbons at around 1403 cm−1 and 1608 cm−1, respectively [48]. These signals resemble the D (1403 cm−1) and G (1608 cm−1) bands, which are characteristics of graphitic carbon nanoparticles. The results suggest that the CNPs have lower crystallinity and high levels of amorphous states.

UV–vis absorption spectrum of CNPs in PBS (pH 7.4) solution featured two major peaks at 230 nm and 270 nm, attributed to π-π* and n-π transitions,[71,72] respectively (Fig. 2 A). Band gap of CNPs was calculated as 4.08 eV. The excitation spectrum (Fig. 2B) was in accord with the absorption spectrum when monitored at the emission wavelength (λem = 360 nm). Fluorescence spectrum exhibited an emission band centered at 362 nm (λex = 270 nm) (Fig. 2C). Fig. 2D depicts the fluorescence spectra of CNPs under different excitation wavelengths. The fluorescence peaks showed an excitation-independent pattern (Fig. 2D).[63] The fluorescence intensity of CNPs increased with the energy of the excitation in the respective spectra.[8] Furthermore, the narrow-bandwidth emissions showed that CNPs had uniform size distribution and surface structure. The fluorescence quantum yield (Φ) of CNPs was 8 % with respect to quinine as a reference.[60] In the literature, it is reported that nitrogen doping, which is done with the electron rich (primer or seconder alkyl) amines (ammonia, ethylene diamine or its derivatives), generally improves the fluorescence properties of the carbon dots. However, the quantum yield of the CNPs is slightly low. We assume that the electron deficient nature of 1,10-phenanthroline plays a critical role in the fluorescence properties of the obtained CNPs. Next, the optical stability of CNPs was tested upon continuous irradiation with white light using LEDs (12 W). The results indicated that the CNPs were optically stable for an hour of irradiation, maintaining more than 92 % of their band intensities (Fig. 2E). Because most commercial fluorophores are quickly photobleached within a few minutes,[46] the optical stability of CNPs was remarkable. The CNPs were resistant to photobleaching. As an aside, the stability of CNPs was also assessed in different concentrations of NaCl solutions. It was noted that the ionic strength of the aqueous solution did not affect the optical properties of the CNPs, which could tolerate high ionic strength (Fig. 2F).

After successful characterization, the pH response of CNPs was investigated with fluorescence spectra between pH 1 and pH 12. Fig. 3 illustrates that fluorescence spectra were pH-sensitive. The fluorescence intensity was significantly increased as the pH of the solution increased from 1 to 12. In the meantime, a concomitant blue shift in the emission band was observed at around pH 5. Note that the fluorescence signal was enhanced at basic pH and protonation of the CNPs resulted in a decrease in the fluorescence signal under acidic conditions. The acquired data indicated the presence of acid/base sensitive functional sites in the CNPs, which were highly effective in the fluorescence signaling. It is assumed that nitrogen atoms, which are present in both the precursor (1,10-phenanthroline) and the CNPs, are acid/base sensitive functional sites. Therefore, it was reasonable to assume that N-doped sites of the CNPs could be responsible for the modulation of fluorescence signal through reversible coordination sequences.

Encouraged by the unique features of CNPs to induce a fluorescent response to pH, we also evaluated the metal ion response of CNPs (3.32 μg/ml) in PBS buffer (pH 7.4). For this purpose, we carried out fluorescence measurements by titrating CNPs with metal ions. We noted that Al3+, Zn2+, Ag+, Au3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Li+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Pt2+, K+,and Na+ (3.33 × 10−6 M) did not affect the fluorescence spectra of CNPs (Fig. 4 A). However, to our delight, the fluorescence emission intensity was increased dramatically upon successive additions of Al(III) and Zn(II) ions, which led to a turn-on response under the same conditions (Fig. 4B). Furthermore, the response of CNPs to Al(III) and Zn(II) ions was concentration-dependent in both cases, as indicated in Fig. 4C and 4D. A progressive increase in the intensity of fluorescence was observed in concentrations between 0.0 and 3.33 × 10−6 M and 0.0–1.66 × 10−6 M for Al(III) and Zn(II) ions, respectively (Fig. 4C and 4D). The addition of Al(III) caused 30-fold whereas Zn(II) induced a 10-fold increase in the fluorescence intensity of CNPs, respectively. As such, Φf of CNPs was increased up to 25 % and 55 % after adding Zn(II) and Al(III), respectively. Benesi-Hildebrand equation was used to determine the binding constants for the interactions of Zn(II) and Al(III) with the CNPs[38,49–54]. In that sense, a plot of (1/I-I0) versus (1/[Zn] or 1/[Al]) was considered. Apparently, the binding constants for Zn(II) and Al(III) were found to be 2.0x103 M−1 and 4.8x103 M−1, respectively (Figures S1 and S2). Experimental data clearly showed that Al3+ and Zn2+ strongly interacted with the CNPs, probably through the lone-pairs of the nitrogen atoms. Therefore, CNPs induced selective response to Al3+ and Zn2+ metal ions. The magnitude of the binding constants posited that Al(III) induced a relatively stronger binding than Zn(II). Afterward, the Job plot analysis revealed that the binding ratio was 1:1 between CNPs and Al(III) or Zn(II) (Figure S3). Based on the acquired luminescence data, the detection limits (LOD) of CNPs were calculated as 3.31 × 10−7 M and 5.75 × 10−8 M for Al(III) and Zn(II), respectively (Figures S4 and S5). It is noteworthy that the maximum tolerable levels of Al(III) and Zn(II), according to the World Health Organization (WHO) are 7.40 × 10−6 M[65] and 7.60 × 10−6 M [73] in drinking water. Indeed, the LODs of CNPs for Al(III) and Zn(II) were lower than the maximum allowed limits of these metal ions. It is also noteworthy that LODs of CNPs are comparable to previously reported chemosensors for Al(III) and Zn(II) (see Table S1). [8,9,11,13,14,20,21,28,45,48,74–79].

Intriguingly, the fluorescence response of CNPs to Al(III) and Zn(II) was completely reversible. The reversibility of the binding of metal ions to CNPs was tested by adding ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) to the solution of CNPs and metal ions. It was envisaged that the addition of metal ions to CNPs solution in PBS would turn-on the luminescence response of CNPs. Addition of EDTA to the above solution would turn-off the luminescence of CNPs since EDTA would chelate the metal ions due to its high affinity, thus, resulting in the initial silent spectra of CNPs. In the end, the luminescence spectra would be restored since metal ions would rebind to CNPs upon successive addition of metal ions to CNPs solution in PBS (pH 7.4). Eventually, everything was realized precisely as in this scenario (Fig. 5 ), and we determined that the metal ion response of CNPs to both Al(III) and Zn(II) was totally reversible.

We also pursued the selectivity of CNPs towards Al(III) and Zn(II) ions by running competition experiments. In the competition experiments, we recorded the fluorescence spectra before and after the addition of a mixture of potential interferents (Ag+, Au3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Li+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Pt2+, K+ and Na+) to CNPs solution in PBS (pH 7.4). As shown in Fig. 6 , the addition of interferents elicited discardable changes in fluorescence spectra of CNPs. In sharp contrast, sequential addition of either Al(III) or Zn(II) markedly increased the luminescence intensity of CNPs. Fig. 6 illustrates the fluorescence spectra of CNPs and Al(III) or Zn(II) in the presence of many interferents. Consistently, CNPs can induce fluorescence response to Al(III) and Zn(II) ions even in the existence of many competing analytes. We believe that the fluorescence enhancement is observed when Al3+ or Zn2+ ions are coordinated to the nitrogens of the CNPs. Upon binding to metal ion, the lone-pairs of nitrogen atoms will not be free due to their involvement in the coordination, and the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process is interrupted (24, 35, 38). Therefore, a drastic change in the fluorescence intensity of CNPs is observed due to the chelation of nitrogen atoms, which results in distinct electronic behavior and charge density.

Gratifyingly, the obtained results implied that CNPs could be used as sensitive, selective, and reversible chemosensors for Al(III), and Zn(II). In fact, these unique features of CNPs to induce a fluorescent response to Al(III), and Zn(II) encouraged us to exploit the potential of the CNPs as chemosensors for real life-samples. To realize this, we opted to use drinking water as the real-life environmental samples, obtained from Aksaray University campus. Different concentrations of metal ions were spiked into water samples and kept 30 min for equilibrium. Then, specific amounts CNPs were added to water samples, and they were screened by recording the fluorescence spectra of the samples. Figure S6 shows the data obtained from the spectrophotometric measurements of water samples with CNPs. The recovery values (in %) were calculated using the standard equation from the actual spiked amounts. Strikingly, the fluorescence emission enhancements were consistent with the spiked concentrations of Al(III) and Zn(II). The recovery values were found between 96.96 % and 103.03 %, which supported that CNPs were capable of sensing Al(III) and Zn(II) in drinking water samples (see Tables S2 and S3).

Finally, we also assessed the efficiency of CNPs in detecting Zn(II) in cosmetic creams.[42] To this purpose, the cosmetic cream sample (0.5 g) was digested by heating slowly in a concentrated acid mixture (HNO3:HClO4, 1:4, v/v). The mixture was cooled and filtered after about 2 h when white fumes were observed. The sample was diluted several times. Finally, specific amounts of the diluted sample, which contains Zn(II), were added to the solution of CNPs to record the changes in the fluorescence spectra. The recorded fluorescence spectra were given in Fig. 7 . Clearly, the CNPs induced a fluorescence response to Zn(II), which was extracted from the cosmetic cream samples. The amount of Zn(II) was determined by luminescence spectra and compared with the results of ICP-MS. The results were tabulated in Table S4. Obviously, the results of luminescence and ICP-MS were in well-agreement. Overall, the obtained results pointed out that CNPs were promising chemosensors for Al(III) and Zn(II) in real-life samples.

The superior optical features of CNPs inspired us to substantiate the premise of CNPs. In order to expand the versatility of the CNPs, we have tested them as bioimaging agents. We surmised that the CNPs could be used in live-cell imaging. In this context, CNPs were evaluated with Human Larynx Squamous Cell Carcinoma (Hep-2) cells. To this end, Hep-2 cells were incubated with CNPs (1 mg/ml) for 6 h before fluorescence microscopy studies. In vitro fluorescence imaging experiments demonstrated that CNPs were not fluorescent in Hep-2 cells. Fortunately, however, it became possible to observe the blue fluorescence of CNPs upon the addition of Al(III) and Zn(II) to cell culture media (Fig. 8 ). Evidently, CNPs easily permeates into Hep-2 cells and allows live-cell imaging of Al(III) or Zn(II) ions. Taken together, CNPs were amenable to in vitro fluorescence imaging of Al(III) and Zn(II) ions by fluorescence microscopy.

Finally, CNPs were incubated with sliced Angelica Officinalis root tissue in the absence and presence of Al(III). As illustrated in Fig. 9 , the root tissue was nonfluorescent after incubation with CNPs (Fig. 9A and 9B). However, it was explicit that CNPs allowed imaging of Al(III) in plant tissue (Fig. 9C).

To sum up, we introduced new N-doped CNPs synthesized by solvothermal method and characterized by spectroscopic methods. It is noteworthy that CNPs have superior features as chemosensors for pH and metal ions. The experimental data attests that CNPs show promise for the detection and imaging of both Al(III), and Zn(II) in real-life samples. The detection limit (LOD) of CNPs were calculated as 3.31 × 10−7 M and 5.75 × 10−8 M for Al(III) and Zn(II), respectively. It is noteworthy that the LODs of CNPs for Al(III) and Zn(II) were lower than the maximum allowed limits of these metal ions. Thus, the versatility of CNPs as chemosensors for Al(III), and Zn(II) was delicately shown by the applications to real-life samples. Last but not least, in vitro imaging of Al(III) and Zn(II) in Hep-2 cells could be achieved successfully. Strikingly, Al(III) imaging in Angelica Officinalis root tissue was also shown successfully. The CNPs are promising as luminescent multianalyte (pH, Al(III) and Zn(II)) sensors. Finally, we expect that the design of congener CNPs may provide promising materials applicable to real-life samples and bioimaging. Further exploration of CNPs is currently underway in our laboratory.
